title: General Assembly Resolution A/RES/ES-11/10
text: Eleventh emergency special sessionAgenda item 5Letter dated 28 February 2014 from the Permanent Representative of Ukraine to the United Nations addressed to the President of the Security Council (S/2014/136)Resolution adopted by the General Assembly on 24 February 2026[without reference to a Main Committee (A/ES-11/L.17)]ES-11/10.Support for lasting peace in UkraineThe General Assembly, Noting with concern that the full-scale invasion of Ukraine by the Russian Federation has persisted for four years and continues to have devastating and long-lasting consequences for Ukraine and for regional and global stability,Reaffirming its strong commitment to the sovereignty, independence, unity and territorial integrity of Ukraine within its internationally recognized borders, extending to its territorial waters,Expressing grave concern at the continued and intensified attacks by the Russian Federation against civilians, civilian objects and critical energy infrastructure, and the grave deterioration of the humanitarian situation,Welcoming the efforts of the United States of America, European countries and other Member States aimed at ending the war,1.Calls for an immediate, full and unconditional ceasefire between the Russian Federation and Ukraine;2.Reiterates its call for a comprehensive, just and lasting peace in line with international law, including the Charter of the United Nations;3.Also reiterates its call for the complete exchange of prisoners of war, the release of all unlawfully detained persons, and the return of all internees and of civilians forcibly transferred or deported, including children, as a significant confidence-building measure;4.Decides to adjourn the eleventh emergency special session of the General Assembly temporarily and to authorize the President of the General Assembly to resume its meetings upon request from Member States.23rd plenary meeting24 February 20262669540304800

title: Issued in GAOR, 80th sess., Suppl. no. 49.
text: Eightieth sessionAgenda items 134 and 145Review of the efficiency of the administrative and financial functioning of the United NationsReport on the activities of the Office of Internal Oversight ServicesResolution adopted by the General Assembly on 30 December 2025[on the report of the Fifth Committee (A/80/568, para. 19)]80/237.Report on the activities of the Office of Internal Oversight ServicesThe General Assembly,IActivities of the Office of Internal Oversight ServicesRecalling its resolutions 48/218 B of 29 July 1994, 54/244 of 23 December 1999, 59/272 of 23 December 2004, 60/259 of 8 May 2006, 63/265 of 24 December 2008, 64/232 of 22 December 2009, 64/263 of 29 March 2010, 65/250 of 24 December 2010, 66/236 of 24 December 2011, 67/258 of 12 April 2013, 68/21 of 4 December 2013, 69/252 and 69/253 of 29 December 2014, 70/111 of 14 December 2015, 71/7 of 27 October 2016, 72/18 of 1 December 2017, 73/275 of 22 December 2018, 74/256 and 74/257 of 27 December 2019, 75/247 of 31 December 2020, 76/241 of 24 December 2021, 77/259 of 30 December 2022 and 78/247 of 22 December 2023,Having considered the report of the Office of Internal Oversight Services on its activities for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025,1.Reaffirms its primary role in the consideration of and action taken on reports submitted to it;2.Also reaffirms its oversight role and the role of the Fifth Committee in administrative and budgetary matters;3.Further reaffirms the independence and the separate and distinct roles of the internal and external oversight mechanisms;4.Recognizes the important roles and operational independence of the oversight bodies, including the Office of Internal Oversight Services of the Secretariat, in contributing to improvements in the effectiveness, transparency and accountability of the Organization;5.Recalls that the Office of Internal Oversight Services of the Secretariat shall exercise operational independence relating to the performance of its internal oversight functions, under the authority of the Secretary-General, in accordance with the relevant resolutions;6.Reaffirms that the purpose of the Office is to assist the Secretary-General in fulfilling his internal oversight responsibilities in respect of the resources and staff of the Organization, with due regard to all mandates of the Office, which are equally relevant;7.Also reaffirms the operational independence of the Office, stresses that its independence and objectivity are vital in ensuring that its internal oversight functions are carried out in a credible, factual and unbiased manner, and further reaffirms the authority of the Office to initiate, carry out and report on any action to fulfil its responsibilities within its mandate and with due regard to all relevant rules, policies and other procedures of the United Nations;8.Further reaffirms that the Office, with respect to its inspection and evaluation functions, shall evaluate the efficiency and the effectiveness of the implementation of the programmes and the legislative mandates of the Organization, in accordance with resolutions of the General Assembly;9.Requests the Secretary-General to ensure that the annual reports of the Office continue to include a brief description of any impairment of its independence;10.Encourages United Nations internal and external oversight bodies to further enhance the level of cooperation with one another, such as through joint work-planning sessions, without prejudice to the independence of each;11.Welcomes continued coordination between the Office of Internal Oversight Services, the Board of Auditors and the Joint Inspection Unit, and notes that the distinct mandates and reporting lines of each oversight body shall not impede the distinct authority of each other;12.Takes note of the report of the Office on its activities for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;13.Requests the Secretary-General to ensure that all relevant resolutions pertaining to the work of the Office are brought to the attention of the relevant managers;14.Also requests the Secretary-General to ensure that all relevant resolutions, including those of a cross-cutting nature, are brought to the attention of relevant managers and that the Office also takes those resolutions into account in the conduct of its activities;15.Stresses that the work of the Office shall continue to remain within its oversight mandate and shall complement, but not substitute, managerial responsibilities over human resources, ethics, workplace conduct or staff engagement policies;16.Emphasizes the importance of the timely completion of the capping review of the Office, encourages the Office to continue drawing on lessons learned from both the capping review and external quality assessments of its individual functions, and looks forward to feedback on the Office’s engagement in any future evaluations to promote the transparency and accountability of the Office;17.Notes the actions of the Office in addressing fraud and presumptive fraud, and urges the Office to further enhance investigations, reporting and follow-up so as to ensure accountability and transparency;18.Also notes the efforts of the Office and its work with the Business Transformation and Accountability Division to enhance the use of evaluation in order to drive improvements in programme performance and mandate delivery and, if applicable, share good practices for achieving organizational results across the United Nations;19.Further notes that there are accepted recommendations that are beyond their targeted date for implementation, also notes that administrative bureaucracy should not slow progress in this regard, and encourages the Office to continue to make recommendations more actionable and to work with the Organization, where appropriate, to ensure the long-term effectiveness and unselective and full implementation of the accepted recommendations;20.Notes the increase in the Office’s investigative case load, further notes the importance of the prioritization of cases that may lead to disciplinary action or financial recovery that pose institutional and reputational risk to the Organization, and requests the Office to include, within existing resources, a rubric regarding the prioritization of investigations in the context of its next report;21.Recalls paragraphs 1 (c) and 2 of its resolution 59/272 and paragraph 4 of its resolution 54/244, and reaffirms that the Under-Secretary-General for Internal Oversight Services may make available to a requesting Member State original versions of the reports of the Office of Internal Oversight Services not submitted to the General Assembly and with consideration for the existing provisions related to confidentiality and due process, where appropriate;22.Welcomes the continued efforts of the Office to implement multilingualism;IIActivities of the Independent Audit Advisory CommitteeRecalling its resolutions 61/275 of 29 June 2007, 64/263, section II of its resolution 65/250, section II of its resolution 66/236, section II of its resolution 67/258, section II of its resolution 68/21, section II of its resolution 69/252, section II of its resolution 70/111, section II of its resolution 71/7, section II of its resolution 72/18, section II of its resolution 73/275, section II of its resolution 74/256, section II of its resolution 75/247, section II of its resolution 76/241 and section II of its resolution 77/259, its resolution 77/278 of 18 April 2023 and section II of its resolution 78/247,Having considered the report of the Independent Audit Advisory Committee on its activities for the period from 1 August 2024 to 31 July 2025,1.Notes with appreciation the work of the Independent Audit Advisory Committee;2.Reaffirms the terms of reference of the Independent Audit Advisory Committee, as set out in the annex to its resolution 61/275, and the mandate of the Committee, as contained in its resolutions 60/248 of 23 December 2005 and 77/278, notes the distinct mandates of the Ethics Office, the Office of Internal Oversight Services and the Independent Audit Advisory Committee, and invites the entities to continue to avoid duplication and overlap in the implementation of their mandates;3.Recalls paragraphs 63 and 68 of the report of the Independent Audit Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to continue to ensure that enterprise risk management and internal control frameworks remain tools under Secretariat authority and do not duplicate or substitute the oversight functions of the Office of Internal Oversight Services or the Committee;4.Requests the Independent Audit Advisory Committee to enhance its future reports by identifying systemic issues and to consider including proposed timelines and benchmarks for the implementation of its recommendations.69th (resumed) plenary meeting30 December 2025

title: Issued in GAOR, 80th sess., Suppl. no. 49.
text: Eightieth sessionAgenda item 136Proposed programme budget for 2026Resolution adopted by the General Assembly on 30 December 2025[on the report of the Fifth Committee (A/80/568, para. 19)]80/242.Questions relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026The General Assembly,Reaffirming its resolutions 41/213 of 19 December 1986, 42/211 of 21 December 1987, 45/248 B, section VI, of 21 December 1990, 55/231 of 23 December 2000, 56/253 of 24 December 2001, 58/269 and 58/270 of 23 December 2003, 59/276, section XI, of 23 December 2004, 60/283 of 7 July 2006, 61/263 of 4 April 2007, 62/236 of 22 December 2007, 63/262 of 24 December 2008, 64/243 of 24 December 2009, 65/259 of 24 December 2010, 66/246 of 24 December 2011, 68/246 of 27 December 2013, 70/247 of 23 December 2015, 72/261 and 72/266 A of 24 December 2017, 72/266 B of 5 July 2018, 73/281 of 22 December 2018, 74/262 of 27 December 2019, 75/252 of 31 December 2020, 76/245 of 24 December 2021, 76/271 of 29 June 2022, 77/262 and 77/267 of 30 December 2022, 77/253 B of 30 June 2023, 78/242 A, 78/252, 78/253 and 78/257 of 22 December 2023, 79/245 A, 79/257 and 79/258 A of 24 December 2024 and 79/258 B of 11 April 2025,Reaffirming also the respective mandates of the Committee for Programme and Coordination and the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions in the consideration of the proposed programme budget,Reaffirming further the role of the General Assembly, through the Fifth Committee, in carrying out a thorough analysis and approval of posts and financial resources, as well as of human resources policies,Having considered the reports of the Secretary-General on the proposed programme budget for 2026, on the revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period, on the implementation of projects financed from the Development Account: fourteenth progress report, on strengthening the effectiveness and impact of the Development Account and on strengthening the effectiveness of the regular programme of technical cooperation, the reports of the Independent Audit Advisory Committee on internal oversight: proposed programme budget for 2026 and internal oversight: revised estimates relating to the proposed programme budget for 2026 and the support account for peacekeeping operations for the 2025/26 period and the related reports of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions,Having also considered the notes by the Secretary-General drawing attention to the reports of the Joint Inspection Unit, as well as the comments of the Secretary-General and of the United Nations System Chief Executive Board for Coordination thereon,1.Reaffirms rule 153 of its rules of procedure;2.Also reaffirms the Regulations and Rules Governing Programme Planning, the Programme Aspects of the Budget, the Monitoring of Implementation and the Methods of Evaluation;3.Further reaffirms the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations;4.Reaffirms its resolution 80/233 of 30 December 2025;5.Endorses the conclusions and recommendations contained in the reports of the Advisory Committee of Administrative and Budgetary Questions, subject to the provisions of the present resolution;6.Reaffirms that the Fifth Committee is the appropriate Main Committee of the General Assembly entrusted with responsibilities for administrative and budgetary matters, and reaffirms the role of the Fifth Committee in carrying out a thorough analysis and approving human and financial resources and policies, with a view to ensuring full, effective and efficient implementation of all mandated programmes and activities and the implementation of policies in this regard;7.Stresses that finance serves as a foundation of and an important element underpinning United Nations governance;8.Recalls its resolution 79/318 of 18 July 2025 on the UN80 Initiative, and welcomes the efforts of the Secretary-General to strengthen the United Nations through structural and programmatic realignment to address duplication, reduce overlap, enhance transparency, strengthen mandate delivery and make the Organization more efficient, effective, impactful and fit for purpose for the people it serves;9.Notes the interconnected nature of the three workstreams of the UN80 Initiative, which seek operational efficiencies and improvements, review of mandate implementation and structural and programmatic realignment of the Organization, stresses in this regard the importance of ensuring close coordination and coherence among the three workstreams so that their outcomes continue to be ambitious and mutually reinforcing and contribute to a comprehensive, accountable, transparent and integrated reform process, and looks forward to tangible outcomes and proposals presented to Member States in 2026 and 2027;10.Recalls paragraphs 4, 6 and 16 of the report of the Advisory Committee, stresses that the UN80 Initiative requires going beyond a one-time reform exercise to also incorporate continuous efforts to ensure long-term effective and efficient mandate delivery across all three pillars of the work of the United Nations, and requests the Secretary-General to pursue synergies to achieve greater efficiency in the management of resources and to report on the outcome of such efforts in his next budget submission;11.Requests the Secretary-General to continue to follow the legislative framework, as agreed by Member States, and the established procedural steps applicable to proposals presented as part of the UN80 Initiative;12.Recognizes the need for indicators for providing quantifiable information on entities’ efforts to drive a culture of efficiency and cost-effectiveness, and requests the Secretary-General to include, in future proposed programme budgets, quantitative indicators on entities’ cost efficiency measures and an analysis of their effectiveness;13.Also recognizes the need to analyse the impact, costs and benefits of the Secretary-General’s reform efforts, and requests the Secretary-General to include, in his future proposed programme budgets, the impact of changes on mandate implementation and performance;14.Recalls paragraph 28 of its resolution 79/257, and requests the Secretary-General to continue applying zero-based budgeting in order to end the high degree of automaticity in future budget preparation and match the proposed requirements with programmes whose mandates are still valid;15.Requests the Secretary-General to maintain the momentum of the UN80 Initiative, continue to identify efficiencies in how each Secretariat entity can enhance the overall effectiveness of the Organization and strengthen budget discipline, by absorbing the resource implications of new mandates within existing resources, where appropriate;16.Recalls paragraph 44 of the report of the Advisory Committee, emphasizes the importance of the rejuvenation of the Secretariat, requests the Secretary-General to include proposals for programmatic realignments and structural changes, including with the aim to reduce or reclassify the overall number of Under-Secretary-General, Assistant Secretary-General, D-2 and D-1 posts markedly in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in the proposed programme budget for 2028;17.Also recalls paragraph 29 of the report of the Advisory Committee, reaffirms paragraphs 19 and 64 of its resolution 79/257, stresses that the implementation of the UN80 Initiative should be supported by a coordinated approach to organizational transformation across the various entities of the Organization, and underscores that internal control and accountability are central principles of management reform, and the importance of strengthening a culture of accountability through the relevant internal oversight and control mechanisms to ensure effective, accountable and transparent delivery of reforms;18.Requests the Secretary-General to ensure effective and efficient utilization of available existing resources and programme delivery by senior managers and programme managers, including through clearly defined results-based performance indicators and benchmarks that provide feedback to senior managers and programme managers and drive improvements on the delivery of mandates;19.Recalls paragraph 74 of the report of the Advisory Committee, encourages continued work on improving administrative and operational effectiveness of the Secretariat, including in coordination with other United Nations system entities, and looks forward to receiving proposals on restructuring Secretariat operations;20.Welcomes the Secretary-General’s proposals on the consolidation and streamlining of support services and structures across the Secretariat, encourages the implementation of such measures to achieve both immediate and long-term financial savings and to foster a more integrated flexible and resilient approach to service delivery, and looks forward to additional proposals from the next phase of work on the proposed programme budget for 2027, while taking into account the different sizes, complexities and operational environments of Secretariat entities;21.Recalls paragraph 71 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to operate under a common approach to scalability for transactional services, including those provided under common administrative platforms, that aligns service demand and related staffing requirements with workload indicators, including variables related to productivity and differences in funding sources;22.Requests the Secretary-General to continue his efforts to achieve transparency and improve the accuracy, comprehensiveness and analysability of the budgetary data, refine the presentation of the proposed programme budget, and to provide complete, clear, comparable and transparent financial data to the General Assembly, including both gross and net requirements, and preliminary cost estimates that are available for the respective year at the time of publication of the proposed programme budget, such as, inter alia, total construction and total recosting estimates, so as to give clarity on the real growth of the budget request to enable Member States to make well-informed decisions;23.Recalls paragraph 23 of its resolution 79/257, and requests the Secretary-General to continue his efforts to provide accurate, adequate and dynamic information related to administrative, budgetary and human resources matters to Member States through the Umoja portal and to continue to enhance Member States’ access to the Umoja system;24.Requests the Secretary-General to update the search and retrieval tools within the e-deleGATE platform to enable Member States to search using keywords across documents and enhance efforts to preserve institutional knowledge;25.Also requests the Secretary-General to consider and implement options to improve the ease of access to e-deleGATE through multi-factor authentication for delegates;26.Recalls paragraph 80 of the report of the Advisory Committee, notes that proposals for relocations should ensure better and more integrated mandate delivery across all aspects of the Organization’s work and contribute to its strategic objectives, while eliminating duplication and saving costs, and emphasizes that all current and future relocation proposals shall be based on functional assessments, including proximity to operations and field presence, demonstrating where work can be delivered most cost-efficiently, and that these assessments shall be provided to Member States;27.Also recalls paragraph 13 of the report of the Advisory Committee, further recalls that multilingualism is a core and fundamental value of the Organization, and emphasizes the critical importance of the UN80 Initiative being leveraged to guarantee its full, effective and efficient implementation;28.Further recalls paragraph VII.22 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to ensure the equal and adequate treatment of all six official languages and to provide an update thereon in his next budget submission;29.Recalls paragraph I.70 of the report of the Advisory Committee, notes current challenges in ensuring full and uninterrupted conference management because of the liquidity situation of the Organization, and encourages the Secretary-General to make additional efforts to provide conference management to meetings contained in the calendar of conferences of the United Nations for 2026 and approved by the General Assembly;30.Urges all Member States to fulfil their financial obligations as set out in the Charter of the United Nations on time, in full and without conditions;31.Requests the Secretary-General to make every effort to facilitate Member States’ payments of their contributions, including in cases when there are circumstances beyond their control in making payments;32.Also requests the Secretary-General to meaningfully engage with Member States in the budget preparation process with respect to the implementation of the recent General Assembly resolutions on budgetary matters and the presentation format of the proposed programme budget;33.Stresses that results-based budgeting and results-based management are mutually supportive management tools and that improved implementation of results-based budgeting enhances both management and accountability in the Secretariat, and encourages the Secretary-General to continue his efforts to improve the presentation of the proposed programme budget and ensure that resources are clearly linked to a results-based budgeting framework and reflect existing mandates and the measures to achieve them;34.Recalls paragraph 28 of its resolution 79/257, underscores that long-term reform can only be achieved through building a culture of efficiency, cost-effectiveness and transparency throughout the Secretariat, and requests the Secretary-General to continue to conduct and strengthen spending reviews;35.Also recalls paragraph 10 of annex I to its resolution 41/213 and paragraph 29 of its resolution 79/257, reiterates the need for a comprehensive solution to the problem of additional expenditures derived from inflation and currency fluctuation, decides not to recost the proposed programme budget as from 2027, pending the comprehensive review of the annual programme budget cycle at the eighty-third session;36.Reaffirms the terms of financial regulation 2.10 and rule 153 of its rules of procedure, urges the Secretary-General to ensure that information is provided in a timely manner and before the adoption of a resolution involving expenditure at the General Assembly and as required, with regard to potential programme budget implications arising from resolutions of the Main Committees or the plenary of the Assembly, including upon request by Member States before the issuance of oral statements on any negotiating text under consideration, as well as from initiatives discussed in subsidiary organs, in order to enable a fully informed decision-making process;37.Recalls paragraph 49 of the report of the Advisory Committee, notes that a significant number of P-2 posts have been subject to reductions under the reform, and requests the Secretary-General to safeguard opportunities for early-career recruitment so as to ensure an open and accessible pathway for highly qualified young and junior professionals;38.Decides that staff members downsized as a result of the UN80 Initiative shall be screened for suitability for vacant posts and positions equally with other internal and external applicants;39.Recalls paragraph 56 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to report on the implementation of the downsizing policy under the agenda item on human resources management, including its impact on staff composition;40.Encourages the Secretary-General to propose cost-saving proposals for post resources involving the abolishment and establishment of posts as a package within a budget section, where appropriate and if applicable;41.Requests the Secretary-General to continue to review how to effectively utilize remaining vacant posts and to report the results of the review by proposing either their retention with clear justification of need, the changing of posts or their abolishment, as appropriate, in his next proposed programme budget;42.Stresses the importance of using realistic and consistent vacancy rate assumptions when formulating post resources budget requests;43.Decides that the staffing table for 2026 shall be as set out in the annex to the present resolution;44.Also decides that a vacancy rate of 10.3 per cent for Professional staff and 4.9 per cent for General Service staff shall be used as a basis for the calculation of the budget for 2026;45.Further decides to further reduce non‑post resources under travel of staff by 3,800,000 United States dollars;46.Recalls paragraph 29 of its resolution 79/257, and decides to further reduce the recosting amount by 8,400,000 dollars;Part IOverall policymaking, direction and coordinationSection 1Overall policymaking, direction and coordination47.Requests the Secretary-General to explore synergies among entities under section 1 to enhance efficiencies in the effective implementation of their specific mandates, and provide an update in the next proposed programme budget;48.Recalls paragraph I.5 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to make every effort to avoid the proliferation of high-level posts in the Organization by integrating functions arising from new and expanded mandates into existing positions, including through double-hatting arrangements, in close consultation with Member States;49.Requests the Advisory Committee to include in its reports a concise summary of the budgetary impact and the financial implications of its recommendations;50.Recalls paragraphs 3 and 4 of section XVIII of its resolution 79/258 A on the decision to provide sufficient funding for the resident coordinator system, looks forward to receiving the refined comprehensive report at the eighty-first session, notes the need to align this process with the UN80 Initiative, and decides to authorize the Secretary-General, exceptionally and without creating a precedent, to enter into commitments with assessment not exceeding 53 million dollars for the period from 1 January to 31 December 2026, in accordance with United Nations financial rules and regulations, to support the funding of the resident coordinator system and requests the Secretary-General to report on the use of the commitment authority in the context of the performance and budget reports;51.Decides against the establishment of the P-2 Associate Programme Management Officer in the Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies;52.Takes note of paragraph I.42 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and decides against the abolishment of the Chief Data Protection and Privacy Officer (D-1);Section 2General Assembly and Economic and Social Council affairs and conference management53.Recognizes the budgetary and operational factors influencing the provision of the interpretation and translation services provided by the Department for General Assembly and Conference Management, and encourages the Secretary-General to continue his efforts to address these challenges and engage with Member States and receive feedback on opportunities to enhance the quality and effectiveness of these services;54.Recalls paragraphs I.56, I.60 and I.61 of the report of the Advisory Committee, decides to increase the workload standards by 20 per cent for all language staff, and requests the Secretary-General to report to the General Assembly in the context of future budget proposals;55.Decides not to abolish one post of Editorial and Desktop Publishing Assistant, Russian (G-5) (General Service (Other level)), in the Russian Text-Processing Unit of the Russian Translation Service, Documentation Division, of the Department for General Assembly and Conference Management, New York (vacant);Part IIPolitical affairsSection 3Political affairs56.Recalls its resolution 78/257, and reaffirms that the Peacebuilding Fund should be managed in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations;57.Decides against the abolishment of one encumbered P-3 post (Political affairs) (subprogramme 1, Prevention, management and resolution of conflicts); and one encumbered General Service post (subprogramme 3, Security Council affairs) (Social affairs);58.Also decides against the abolishment of the Senior Political Affairs Officer, P-5 (subprogramme 2), in the Electoral Assistance Division;59.Further decides not to abolish one D-1 post of Chief, Security Council Practices and Charter Research Branch;60.Decides not to abolish one P-3 Political Affairs Officer, Liaison Team, in Nairobi, under subprogramme 1;Section 4Disarmament61.Takes note of paragraph II.107 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and approves the proposed redeployment of posts to Vienna;Section 5Peacekeeping operations62.Decides to maintain and redeploy the post of Assistant Secretary-General for Rule of Law and Security Institutions as the Assistant Secretary-General post in the Office for Mine Action and Police reporting directly to the Under-Secretary-General for Peace Operations and responsible for overseeing the operations of the Police Division and the Mine Action Service and undertaking other functions for the Under-Secretary-General;Section 6Peaceful uses of outer space63.Takes note of paragraph II.172 of the report of the Advisory Committee, decides to abolish the P-2 Associate Scientific Affairs Officer, and further decides against the abolishment of the P-3 Political Affairs Officer;Part IVInternational cooperation for developmentSection 9Economic and social affairs64.Decides against the abolishment of one D-1 post in subprogramme 4, Statistics;65.Recalls paragraph IV.15 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and further decides not to approve the proposed abolishment of three P-2/1 posts (vacant);66.Decides not to abolish one Economic Affairs Officer (P-5) in subprogramme 9;67.Also decides not to abolish the vacant P-4 Geospatial Information Officer in subprogramme 4 that supports the Committee of Experts on Global Geospatial Information Management as the secretariat;Section 11United Nations system support for the African Union’s Agenda 2063: The Africa We Want68.Recalls that the development of Africa is an established priority of the United Nations, and reaffirms its commitment to addressing the unique needs of Africa;Section 14Environment69.Takes note of paragraph IV.93 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and decides to relocate the P-5 Senior Programme Management Officer from Washington, D.C., to Paris;Section 16International drug control, crime and terrorism prevention and criminal justice70.Recalls paragraph IV.135 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and decides to abolish the Associate Crime Prevention and Criminal Justice Officer (P-2) post under subprogramme 3, and the Associate Drug Control and Crime Prevention Officer (P-2) post under subprogramme 9;Section 17UN-Women71.Decides against the abolishment of one D-1 Chief of Economic Empowerment, subprogramme 2;Part VRegional cooperation for developmentSection 18Economic and social development in Africa72.Decides against the abolishment of two Local level/Security Service encumbered posts in programme support;Section 19Economic and social development in Asia and the Pacific73.Recalls paragraphs V.39 (j) and V.45 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and decides not to abolish one P-5 post of Senior Economic Affairs Officer, in the Subregional Office for North and Central Asia in Almaty, Kazakhstan;74.Decides not to approve the proposed abolishment of one P-3 post of Translator, Chinese (encumbered);Section 21Economic and social development in Latin America and the Caribbean75.Decides not to abolish one Economic Affairs Officer (P-3) in subprogramme 11, Subregional activities in Central America, Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Haiti and Mexico;76.Also decides not to abolish one Economic Affairs Officer (P-4) in subprogramme 12, Subregional activities in the Caribbean;Section 23Regular programme of technical cooperation77.Takes note of paragraphs 11 and 27 of the report of the Advisory Committee;Part VIIGlobal communicationsSection 28Global communications78.Recalls paragraph 62 of its resolution 79/257 and paragraph VII.4 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to complete the independent and impartial review of the Department of Global Communications by the end of 2026 without any further delay, reiterates that the review itself should be within existing resources and be aimed at achieving greater efficiency gains, and also requests the Secretary-General to provide the outcome of the review for the consideration of the General Assembly at the main part of its eighty-first session in the context of the proposed programme budget for 2027;79.Also recalls paragraph VII.10 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and looks forward to proposals on the restructuring of the Department of Global Communications, at both the Headquarters and regional levels, including United Nations information centres, in the next proposed programme budget;80.Takes note of paragraph VII.23 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and approves the proposed redeployment of posts to Vienna and Nairobi;81.Decides not to abolish one G-6, General Service (Other level) post of Communications Assistant, Russian Unit, Web Services Section, News and Media Division (subprogramme 2) (encumbered);82.Also decides not to abolish the following posts under subprogramme 2:(a)One P-3 Public Information Officer (Arabic);(b)One P-3 Public Information Officer (Chinese);(c)One P-3 Public Information Officer (Russian);Part VIIICommon support servicesSection 29ADepartment of Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance83.Recalls paragraph VIII.8 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and further requests the Secretary-General to continue his efforts to provide adequate, accurate and timely supplementary information to the General Assembly in order to enhance transparency and efficiency of deliberations regarding the information available prior to the formal meeting of each agenda item;84.Welcomes with appreciation the existing practice of informal sharing of consultants’ reports on management-related issues by the Secretary-General with Member States, and encourages the Secretary-General to continue this practice;85.Takes note of paragraphs VIII.20 and VIII.21 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and decides to approve the proposed redeployments to Bonn;Section 29BDepartment of Operational Support86.Recalls paragraph VIII.35 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to propose further options to consolidate the Organization’s support functions, including through enhanced use of common administrative platforms, in his next budget report;87.Also recalls paragraph VIII.63 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to incorporate the views of client entities at the design stage of common administrative platforms and to conduct client-based performance assessments once implemented to ensure that common administrative platforms remain responsive, add value and are fit for purpose, and to report thereon to the General Assembly;88.Takes note of paragraph VIII.60 of the report of the Advisory Committee, decides against the abolishment of two D-1 posts at this time, and requests the Secretary General to review the necessity of these posts in the context of the proposed programme budget for 2028;Section 29COffice of Information and Communications Technology89.Recalls paragraphs VIII.74 and VIII.96 of the reports of the Advisory Committee, and requests the Secretary-General to enhance functional reporting between the information and communications technology Chiefs of United Nations entities and the Office of Information and Communications Technology, in a manner that complements and fully respects existing reporting mechanisms, and avoiding the creation of parallel or isolated structures, and update the General Assembly in the context of its next report on the information and communications technology strategy;90.Also recalls paragraph VIII.82 of the report of the Advisory Committee, reaffirms the importance of increasing transparency and clarity of information and communications technology expenditure, and therefore requests the Secretary-General to provide consolidated information on information and communications technology budgets and expenditures, including the intention to utilize information and communications technology resources more efficiently, to the General Assembly during its next session;91.Further recalls paragraph VIII.61 of the report of the Advisory Committee, reiterates the need for a holistic vision for the future use of data centres and cloud services across the Organization, and invites the Secretary-General to provide information on possible consolidation measures for existing data centres and the potential cost-efficiencies available in the proposed programme budget for 2027;Section 29DAdministration, Nairobi92.Decides to retain two General Service positions under subprogramme 3, Support services;Part XJointly financed administrative activities and special expensesSection 32Special expenses93.Decides to authorize a commitment authority without assessment on Member States in an amount not exceeding 52,611,600 dollars under section 32, Special expenses, to support one-time costs in 2026, which enables the implementation of post abolishments and post relocations, including for special political missions, and all the temporary posts proposed by the Secretary-General for transitional capacities;94.Requests the Secretary-General to keep to an absolute minimum the amount required from the one-time commitment authority, and emphasizes that the commitment authority shall not require any additional assessments from Member States;Part XICapital expendituresSection 33Construction, alteration, improvement and major maintenance95.Decides to reduce resources for the United Nations Office at Geneva by 1,247,400 dollars;96.Also decides to reduce resources for Headquarters by 4,102,100 dollars;Part XIISafety and securitySection 34Safety and security97.Approves the Secretariat’s share in the jointly financed security budget, and encourages the Secretary-General to ensure that the Department of Safety and Security is implementing structural, operational and functional efficiencies within its budget while safeguarding its mandated functions;98.Emphasizes the strategic and cross-departmental nature of the Emergency Response Team and Operational Resilience Section established in 2024, and decides not to approve the redeployment of the Team and Section, with all 17 posts, from executive direction and management to subprogramme 2, Field operations;99.Recalls paragraph XII.12 of the report of the Advisory Committee, and stresses the importance of operational fitness of uniformed personnel;Part XIIIDevelopment AccountSection 35Development account100.Takes note of paragraphs 17 and 22 of the report of the Advisory Committee;Part XIVStaff assessmentIncome section 2General income101.Notes with concern the significant variance between the general income and the approved estimates in 2024, and requests the Secretary-General to take measures to present
